name: ACM Conference (sigconf)
notes: ACM sigconf format. Uses acmart class. Double-blind by default. 12-page limit excluding refs. Requires CCS concepts and keywords. Use \begin{CCSXML} and \keywords{}.
writing_guide:
TONE: Formal but slightly more accessible than IEEE. Active voice preferred ('We design...', 'Our system achieves...'). Technical precision is expected but explain concepts for a broad CS audience, not just subfield specialists.

ABSTRACT: 200-250 words. State motivation, problem, approach, key results (with numbers), and broader impact. Must be self-contained. Include keywords after abstract.

STRUCTURE: Separate 'Evaluation' and 'Results' sections are common (not combined). Include a 'Threats to Validity' or 'Limitations' subsection. 'Discussion' section should cover implications beyond the immediate results. Reproducibility is paramount: describe experimental setup in enough detail to replicate.

CITATIONS: 30-60 references typical. Cite ACM DL papers when available. Use \citet for narrative ('Smith et al. [5] showed...') and \citep for parenthetical. Related Work should be thorough and organized by theme, not chronologically.

FIGURES/TABLES: 6-10 display items. Include system architecture or workflow diagrams. Tables should compare against baselines with statistical significance markers. Consider including a motivating example figure early.

REVIEWER EXPECTATIONS: Reproducibility (link to code/data if possible, describe artifact). Clear threat-to-validity analysis. User studies need IRB mention. Statistical significance tests required for empirical claims.

DO NOT: Reveal author identity (double-blind). Do not skip CCS concepts and keywords. Do not present results without statistical testing. Do not ignore artifact evaluation expectations.
